chapters.title: A legacy of love
chapters.text:
Long after slavery was abolished, a Black woman named Ruth Middleton lived and worked in South Carolina.

Life was hard for her and for others in her position. They worked long hours for very little pay and had few legal rights. During this time, African Americans were searching for a better life away from the harsh conditions of the South. Many headed north, and Ruth was one of them, hoping for more chances and a safer place to live.

Ruth arrived in Philadelphia carrying a small sack marked with stitched words. The family story says that Rose, Ruth's great grandmother, gave the bag to her daughter Ashley when Ashley was about to be sold and torn away from her mother.

Even under difficult conditions, always find creative ways to stay connected to the people you love.

By following the bag as it passed from one generation to the next until it reached Ruth, the book opens a window into the private lives of people trapped by the transatlantic slave trade. You will see their struggles and the ways they built family bonds and kept those ties alive in spite of brutal conditions. Let's begin.

chapters.title: A life hidden from the record
chapters.text:
Rose, Ruth's great grandmother, lived in slavery in Charleston, South Carolina, a time and place where daily life was unimaginably hard, especially for people held in bondage.

Charleston glittered with wealth, and that shine was built on the labor of enslaved people.

Beauty and wealth produced through suffering keep injustice and moral harm alive.

Rose's daily life revolved around the Martins, a wealthy Charleston family. They were part of the elite who had made their fortune from trade and from owning people. For Rose, that meant doing whatever her owners demanded, with almost no say in her own life.

The written record of Rose's life is thin. The people who enslaved her listed her as property, with a price. They did not tell us what Rose hoped for or what she feared. Still, it is easy to picture her rising early, working long hours, always under the watchful eyes of her owners. The Martins saw themselves as kind and paternal figures, but they were anything but that. They controlled every part of Rose's life and the lives of the other people they enslaved, often using threats and cruelty.

Rose lived through relentless hardship, but she found comfort in the strong bonds among the enslaved, who shared stories, songs, and dreams of freedom under the cover of night. Despite heavy surveillance, Rose and her peers built a quiet culture of resistance, passing down African traditions and teaching their children to read and write in secret, since the law forbade it.

Many details of Rose's life are lost to history. Curious historians keep digging into how her story ended and what she went through. Even so, Rose was an extraordinary woman. With almost everything taken from her, she held on to the thing that mattered most, her love for her daughter Ashley.

chapters.title: How the slave trade tore families apart
chapters.text:
Like any mother, Rose loved her daughter Ashley more than anything, and being together gave them both comfort.

Then Rose's life changed when her owner, Robert Martin, died. In that era, the death of an enslaver often led to the sale of the people they had enslaved. Rose feared that she and Ashley would be torn apart. She had seen many families split up and knew the pain and trauma it caused.

The greatest threat that enslaved people faced was separation from their families.

Rose's life with Ashley would have been full of small joys and everyday moments. She would have cared for Ashley, taught her about life, shared stories, shown her love each day, and watched her grow. But now, a tragedy was coming, and Rose felt it.

She decided to make a special sack for Ashley, a bag that would be more than just a place to store things. It would carry her love and her family history if they had to part.

Rose chose the items in the bag with care. She put in a worn dress, a practical piece of clothing Ashley could wear. She also added pecans, a valuable food that could keep Ashley fed in hard times. Most importantly, Rose cut off a lock of her own hair and placed it in the bag, a deeply personal item, a piece of herself meant to comfort Ashley and remind her of her mother's love.

Instead of giving up, always look for the bright side in any situation.

The sack became more than an ordinary bag. It was a mother's goodbye gift, a symbol of her love, her strength, and her hope for a way to stay connected to Ashley, even when they were far apart.

As the sack passed from generation to generation, each person who held it felt a link to Rose and Ashley. They learned about the women's strength and the hard times they had lived through.

chapters.title: Tokens of strength and identity
chapters.text:
As mentioned earlier, one of the items in Ashley's sack was a small handful of pecans. Rose chose them for their value. They could be eaten for strength or planted to grow new life. Rose wanted them to be her silent words of encouragement, her way of providing for the days ahead.

Enslaved people had to be resourceful, and even something as small as pecans could mean a lot. When food was scarce, or was used as a kind of currency, pecans were useful because they could be stored or traded. They stood in for wealth and power at a time when both were rare. Pecans also came from trees that grew far away and were seen as exotic. It is possible that Rose saved them from her kitchen work, or got them in a trade.

Along with the pecans, there was a beautiful hand woven cloth in the sack. The cloth gave a hint about the family's experience of separation. Throughout history, people have used clothing to show their identity and background.

In places like the United States, dresses were a kind of language. They reflected social rank, marking clearly who was in charge and who was not. Along with skin color, hair texture, and facial features, clothing added to the divide. For enslaved people, what they wore often showed their low status. It was usually rough and plain, picked out by owners to underline who they belonged to. Clothing like this was a visible mark of a system built to keep enslaved people under control.

Choose your clothing with care. It can say a great deal about your background and identity.

Even under that harsh system, the enslaved Rose found ways to get fine fabrics and clothing, sometimes through long term ties with white men or through informal markets and pawnshops. She gave the cloth to her daughter as a goodbye gift.

The history of Africans in America is brutal, but we have made art out of pain.

chapters.title: A pretty town with a brutal trade
chapters.text:
Let's pause and look at the town where Rose and Ashley lived.

Charleston, South Carolina, has a dark and unforgettable history tied to slavery. The town was lively and rich, but its wealth was built on the brutal trade in human lives. Africans were brought there in chains, and their forced labor created fortunes for the town's elite. The wealth came from slave trading and from owners who made enslaved people work on plantations.

The town started small, but by the 1800s it had become a major center for buying and selling enslaved people. Visitors from other places were shocked by what they saw. They could not believe that a town so beautiful could tolerate something so cruel, where even children were auctioned off to the highest bidder.

Along with the slave trade, the town controlled its enslaved population with strict laws and harsh punishments. There was a building called the WorkHouse, where enslaved people were sent if they tried to run away or broke a rule. Inside its walls, they faced punishments meant to frighten them and keep them working without question.

Communities can only flourish when they are built on strong moral principles and mutual respect.

As the city's wealth grew, so did the pain and suffering of the people it enslaved. The town's leaders and wealthy residents did not want to face the evil they were part of. They tried to convince themselves that the enslaved were happy, or that they were better off in Charleston than anywhere else. Deep down, they knew that was not true.

Charleston had a hard stone known as the auction block, where lives were treated as objects. There, enslaved people were sold to the highest bidder, and Ashley was sold at the same block.

Watching her daughter sold for a few hundred dollars, a price that can never match a human life, was a cruel experience for her mother.

Nearly 40 percent of the enslaved Africans brought to North America arrived through the port of Charleston, on about a thousand separate ships.

chapters.title: A road toward freedom
chapters.text:
Ashley was only nine when she was separated from her mother and sold to a new owner, who took her to a cotton plantation far from her birthplace.

Life on the plantation was hard. Ashley had to work long hours in the cotton fields under the hot sun. Even at that age, she had to pick cotton like an adult.

The work was tiring, and the overseers were strict. They punished any enslaved person who did not work fast enough or tried to rest. Ashley missed her mother deeply and often cried herself to sleep.

As Ashley grew older, she learned how to survive on the plantation. She made friends with other enslaved people, and they looked out for each other. They shared stories and songs that helped them push their troubles aside. Ashley was especially good at sewing, a skill she had picked up from her mother. She mended clothes for others in her spare time, simply because she loved it.

Years went by, and the Civil War began. The war brought hope to many enslaved people, including Ashley. They heard rumors about freedom and about Abraham Lincoln, the president who wanted to end slavery. The war made life on the plantation even harder, though. Food grew scarce, and the overseers became more brutal, afraid of losing the people they controlled.

Then the war finally ended, and slavery was abolished. Ashley was now a young woman, free at last. Freedom came with new challenges, too. She had no money, no home, and no education. Like many formerly enslaved people, she had to build her life from nothing.

Welcome new beginnings with courage and vision. They clear the path to a brighter future.

Ashley decided to stay in South Carolina. She found work as a seamstress, using the skills her mother had taught her. The work was hard for little pay, but Ashley was grateful to be free and to make her own choices. She always remembered her mother and the others she had lost.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
The sack's journey started when Rose, a mother facing separation from her family because of slavery, made it for her daughter Ashley.

Years later, the sack made its way to Ruth, Ashley's granddaughter. For everyone who saw or used the sack, it told a story of struggle, love, and endurance.

While there are many lessons in this story, one stands out. You never know how far your kind gestures and good deeds will travel.

As you remember this sack and its story, let it inspire you to keep the values of love and hope alive, no matter how hard life becomes.

Try this

Record your family stories. Ask older relatives to share their experiences and write them down.

Want to learn more about slavery and emancipation without reading a long book. Check out titles like The Black Jacobins and The 1619 Project on the Headway app.

Read it till the end?
